downloadSentinel:
Core Pathway A satisfied. I verified Core Radiology coverage in the uploaded Core PDF under GI → Spleen → Splenic trauma, visible page markers GI:170–171.

Whole-source coverage audit completed from full Core pages and full article text.
Core Summary attached; derived from full Core coverage, not from validation digest.
Card generation used full-source review, not digest summary.

Core Summary: Core emphasizes that splenic trauma is best assessed on portal venous phase because arterial heterogeneous splenic enhancement can mimic or mask injury; lacerations appear as linear/branching decreased attenuation on contrast CT; hematomas may be subcapsular or intraparenchymal; active extravasation enlarges or changes on delayed imaging, unlike contained pseudoaneurysm/AVF; pseudoaneurysm has high rupture risk; and the 2018 AAST spleen injury scale incorporates vascular injury and active bleeding.

Total cards to export: 39
Expected TSV lines: 39

Download:

CORE_EVIDENCE_FILE_BEGIN
CORE_EVIDENCE_STATUS: USED
CORE_SOURCE_BASIS: Uploaded Core Radiology PDF, “Core Radiology: A Visual Approach to Diagnostic Imaging,” GI chapter, Spleen section, “Splenic trauma,” visible page markers GI:170–171; exact PDF absolute page numbers not separately exposed.
CORE_FACTS_USED:

- The spleen is the most commonly injured abdominal organ in blunt trauma.
- Splenectomy increases subsequent sepsis risk, supporting splenic preservation/conservative management.
- Portal venous phase is required for splenic parenchymal injury evaluation because arterial heterogeneous enhancement can mask or mimic injury.
- Splenic hematoma is hypoattenuating relative to enhanced spleen and hyperattenuating relative to unenhanced spleen; usually subcapsular, less often intraparenchymal.
- Splenic laceration is best seen on contrast-enhanced CT as a linear or branching area of decreased attenuation.
- Active extravasation increases in size or attenuation on delayed imaging due to pooling of extravasated blood.
- Pseudoaneurysm/AVF are contained vascular injuries that do not enlarge on delayed imaging; splenic arteriography distinguishes pseudoaneurysm from AVF.
- Pseudoaneurysm is caused by intima/media injury and is contained only by adventitia, with high rupture risk if untreated.
- 2018 AAST spleen injury scale incorporates CT vascular injuries, including active bleeding, pseudoaneurysm, and AVF.
- AAST grade IV includes intraparenchymal vascular injury/contained active bleeding or central-vessel laceration with >25% devascularization; grade V includes extraparenchymal active bleeding or shattered spleen.
CORE_DERIVED_CARDS:
- Summary field across all exported cards.
- Mechanism cards on portal venous phase evaluation, delayed-phase extravasation-vs-pseudoaneurysm distinction, and pseudoaneurysm rupture risk.
- Boards trap cards on arterial-phase mimic/masking pitfall and delayed imaging for vascular injury.
- High-yield cards on CT phase strategy, AAST-OIS framework, vascular injury/intervention pivots, and reporting grade.
- UNKNOWN card reasoning where Core facts supported laceration, hematoma, extravasation, pseudoaneurysm, and AAST grading interpretation.
CORE_LIMITATIONS:
- Core did not provide the full STATdx-specific selected-image captions; those were derived from the supplied STATdx article/image block.
- Core did not provide the STATdx numeric hemoperitoneum/perisplenic clot HU thresholds, FAST/DPL false-negative percentages, polytrauma proportions, delayed reimaging timing, or nonoperative-management failure rates; those were taken from the supplied STATdx article.
CORE_EVIDENCE_FILE_END

RADPRIMER_CARD_TSV_DOWNLOAD_READY

EXPECTED_ROWS: 39
